request.question: The man who the novel "Strandloper" is based on lived with what community after being given up for dead?

title: Fosters, Ohio
Count No Man Happy: A Byzantine Fantasy
The Palm-Wine Drinkard
Jornada del Muerto
Kent Curtis
Invisible Monsters
Strandloper (novel)
Pauma massacre
William Buckley (convict)
Cythera (novel)
sentences: Fosters (sometimes given as Foster) is an unincorporated community in southern Warren County, Ohio, United States.
It straddles the Little Miami River in Deerfield and Hamilton Townships.
It is located about two miles southwest of Hopkinsville, two miles west of Maineville, and two miles northeast of Twenty Mile Stand just off U.S. Route 22/State Route 3, the 3C Highway.
(In the 1930s, the State of Ohio erected a new high bridge over the river that bypassed the community.)
The community is in the Kings Local School District and is served by the Maineville post office.
The settlement was originally called "Foster's Crossing", and had its own post office.
It is in the Little Miami telephone exchange.
Governor Jeremiah Morrow once lived there.
Count No Man Happy: A Byzantine Fantasy is a novel or fictionalized biography published in 2011 by author Paul Kastenellos.
Count No Man Happy recounts the life of the Byzantine emperor Constantine VI who lived in the last years of the eighth century CE.
Although a novel with elements of fantasy, it is also factually correct biography.
The book’s title is a quote from the ancient historian Herodotus: “Count no man happy until he is dead.”
The unhappy life of Constantine was one of religious extremism, court intrigue, family feuding, and a two front war.
This reality is relieved by Constantine's dreams in which he is comforted by Beth, a mid twentieth century model inspired by the smile of the pinup model Bettie Page.
The Palm-Wine Drinkard (subtitled "and His Dead Palm-Wine Tapster in the Dead's Town") is a novel published in 1952 by the Nigerian author Amos Tutuola.
The first African novel published in English outside of Africa, this quest tale based on Yoruba folktales is written in a modified Yoruba English or Pidgin English.
In it, a man follows his brewer into the land of the dead, encountering many spirits and adventures.

The novel has always been controversial, inspiring both admiration and contempt among Western and Nigerian critics, but has emerged as one of the most important texts in the African literary canon, translated into over a dozen languages.

The name Jornada del Muerto is translated loosely from Spanish, historically referring to it as the "Journey of the Dead Man", though the modern literal translation is closer to "The Working Day of the Dead".
As a geographic name, "Jornada del Muerto" is the desert region the Conquistadors had to cross to make it from Las Cruces to Socorro, New Mexico.

As a name-place, "Jornada del Muerto" is a loose translations of "single day's journey of the dead man" hence "route of the dead man") in the U.S. state of New Mexico was the name given by the Spanish conquistadors to the Jornada del Muerto Desert basin, and the particularly dry 100 mi stretch of a route through it.
Marvin Kent Curtis (October 10, 1890 – December 24, 1957) was an American novelist, illustrator, composer, yachtsman, and teacher.
Curtis served in World War I as an aviator with the Royal Air Force, was shot down, reported dead, and held prisoner of war until the war's end.
Based on his war experiences, he authored "The Tired Captains", a novel centered on a group of World War I pilots.
Curtis was of the "Lost Generation," Americans born in the 1890s who came of age during World War I.
He lived intermittently in Paris during the 1920s.
Curtis published primarily boys’ adventure stories set in the places where he lived: the North Woods of Minnesota and the islands off the Florida coast.
Invisible Monsters is a novel by Chuck Palahniuk, published in 1999.
It is his third novel to be published, though it was his second written novel (after "Insomnia: If You Lived Here, You'd Be Home Already").
The novel was originally supposed to be Palahniuk's first novel to be published, but it was rejected by the publisher for being too disturbing.
After the success of his novel "Fight Club", "Invisible Monsters" was given a second chance, and a revised version of it was published.
The first edition was released in paperback in 1999, and on June 11, 2012, it was published in hardcover, in a revised edition titled "Invisible Monsters Remix" (ISBN  ).
The novel has been adapted into a graphic novel by comic artist KGZ, a.k.a. Gabor Kiss.
Strandloper is a novel by English writer Alan Garner, published in 1996.
It is loosely based on the story of a Cheshire labourer, William Buckley.
The historical figures of Edward Stanley and John Batman also appear as characters in the novel.
The Pauma massacre occurred in December 1846, north of Escondido, California.
Luiseño Indians killed eleven Mexicans, Californio lancers who had stolen horses from them.
The action was related to a series of regional conflicts during the Mexican–American War and followed the Battle of San Pasqual in California.
Fundamentally, it was also related to the appropriation of Mission Luiseno land from the Luiseño after the successful mission with a population of 3,000 was secularized in 1833.
Gov. José Figueroa had granted the Luiseño three pueblos including Las Flores and San Pascual.
Pío Pico was to hold the mission land in trust for the government as administrator pending a decision on what to do with it.
Eventually, Pico took Las Flores as his personal ranch.
Pico was the man who led the Mexicans at the battle of San Pascual.

The Kumeyaay Indians lived at San Pascual, though the Kumeyaay were from the next mission, San Diego, they also viewed Pio Pico with distrust, and their sympathies for the Americans may have been decisive, given their knowledge of the terrain in which they lived.
The Mexicans lost and afterwards took 11 horses from a nearby Luiseño community, which then retaliated by killing 11 Mexican Californio soldiers at the Massacre of Pauma.
William Buckley (178030 January 1856) was an English convict who was transported to Australia, escaped, was given up for dead, and lived in an Aboriginal community for many years.
Cythera is the fourth novel by British science fiction author Richard Calder, and was first published in 1998.

While it is not explicitly advertised as a continuation of Calder's previous novels it does appear to be set in the same universe as his 'Dead...' trilogy, as it references some of the background of the earlier novels and features two supporting characters - Kito and Mosquito - who were introduced in "Dead Girls", though knowledge of these novels is not required to understand this stand-alone novel.
Due to the timeline given the events of "Cythera" appear to take place before those of "Dead Girls", making this a prequel of sorts.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: The man who the novel "Strandloper" is based on lived with what community after being given up for dead?
hop1_docs: Strandloper (novel) | Strandloper is a novel by English writer Alan Garner, published in 1996.  It is loosely based on the story of a Cheshire labourer, William Buckley.  The historical figures of Edward Stanley and John Batman also appear as characters in the novel.

Buzz Aldrin, hvor ble det av deg i alt mylderet? | Buzz Aldrin, What Happened To You in All The Confusion?  (Original title: "Buzz Aldrin, hvor ble det av deg i alt mylderet?")  is a novel by the Norwegian author Johan Harstad, published in 2005.  The book deals with a thirty-year-old gardener, Mattias, near obsessed with the thought of being second best, the greatest number two, much to his girlfriend's grievance.  He looks upon astronaut Buzz Aldrin, the second man on the Moon, as his role model, and tries to live up to what he thinks Aldrin would do in any given situation.  He has an extraordinary talent as a singer, but refuses to become a singer in his friend's semi-famous band, arguing that "not everyone wants to be a leader, some just want to be the secretary.  Not everyone wants to star in a movie, some just want to watch the movie."  After losing both his girlfriend and his job, he accidentally ends up on the Faroe Islands, where he meets people with the same ideals as himself, living in a psychiatric halfway house.  Using the lunar-like landscape of the Faroe Islands as a backdrop, the novel deals with their attempt at finding a balance between being second best and anonymous without going into total isolation.

William Buckley (convict) | William Buckley (178030 January 1856) was an English convict who was transported to Australia, escaped, was given up for dead, and lived in an Aboriginal community for many years.

Strandloper peoples | The Strandlopers are a San-derived people who live by hunting and gathering food along the beaches of south-western Africa, originally from the Cape Colony to the Skeleton Coast.

Allan Stein | Allan Stein is a 1999 novel by Matthew Stadler.  Its epigraph is a quotation from writer Gertrude Stein: ""What is the use of being a boy if you grow up to become a man, what is the use?""

Circle Series | The Circle Series, formerly known as the Circle Trilogy until the novel "Green" was released in September 2009, is a tetralogy of spiritually inspired novels by American author Ted Dekker, written mostly in 2004, about a man named Thomas Hunter who, after a head injury, wakes up in an alternate reality every time he goes to sleep.  The stakes are raised when he realizes that a deadly virus is about to be unleashed in the world, and that the other earth is also being threatened with catastrophe.  The pace quickens as links and parallels between the two worlds are revealed, and the clock begins to run down for both worlds.

Nowhere Man (Hemon novel) | Nowhere Man is a novel by Aleksandar Hemon, published in 2002 and named after the Beatles song "Nowhere Man".  The novel (subtitled "The Pronek Fantasies") centers around the character of Jozef Pronek, a Bosnian refugee, who was already the subject of Hemon's novella "Blind Jozef Pronek & Dead Souls" published in his short story collection "The Question of Bruno" (2000).
hop1_titles: Buzz Aldrin, hvor ble det av deg i alt mylderet?
response.summary_1: Passage 1: "Strandloper ... is loosely based on the story of a Cheshire labourer, William Buckley." Passage 3: William Buckley "was an English convict ... escaped, was given up for dead, and lived in an Aboriginal community for many years." Bridge entity: William Buckley connects the novel to the fact that the man lived with an Aboriginal community after being given up for dead; use those quoted phrases as retrieval anchors.
response.hop2_query: William Buckley given up for dead lived in an Aboriginal community
hop2_docs: William Buckley (convict) | William Buckley (178030 January 1856) was an English convict who was transported to Australia, escaped, was given up for dead, and lived in an Aboriginal community for many years.
William Cooper (Aboriginal Australian) | William Cooper ( 1860 – 1941) was an Australian Aboriginal political activist and community leader.

Michael Buckley (Internet celebrity) | Michael John Buckley (born June 8, 1975) is an American Internet celebrity, comedian and vlogger.  Noted for his vlog "What the Buck?!" , Buckley comments on popular culture events and celebrities.  He also maintains one of YouTube's most popular entertainment channels.  Buckley "broke all records" of YouTube ratings when four of his shows ended up on the week's ten top-rated videos.  Buckley has appeared in magazines and newspapers such as "The New York Times" discussing Internet entrepreneurship and "The Advocate" discussing homophobia on the Internet.  On March 18, 2008, he won a 2007 YouTube Awards for best commentary with the video "LonelyGirl15 is Dead!"

Aboriginal Land Trust | In Australia, an Aboriginal Land Trust (ALT) is a type of non-profit organisation that holds the freehold title to an area of land on behalf of a community of Aboriginal Australians.  The land has been legally granted to a community by the government under a perpetual lease, usually after the community makes a formal claim of traditional ownership.  Land granted under Aboriginal title is inalienable; it can not be bought, sold, traded or given away.  The Land Trust is the organisation appointed by the community to legally hold the title deeds.

Deadly Awards | The Deadly Awards, commonly known simply as The Deadlys, were an annual celebration of Australian Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander achievement in music, sport, entertainment and community.  The first Deadlys were held in 1995, at the Boomalli Artist Co-op in the Redfern suburb of Sydney.  The Deadly Awards originated as the Deadly Sounds music and culture radio show at the Boomalli Aboriginal Artists Co-op in Redfern in 1993.  They stemmed from an idea of the late Gavin Jones (1966-2014).  Later venues included The Metro Theatre, the Hard Rock Café, Home in Darling Harbour and Fox Studios; since 2001 the event has been held at the Sydney Opera House, hosted by Vibe Australia and broadcast by SBS Television.  There were later additional venues in other states.

Beeliar Wetlands | The Beeliar Wetlands are made up of two chains of lakes and wetlands that run parallel to the west coast of Western Australia.  They are situated on the coastal plan between the Darling Escarpment and the Indian Ocean.  "Beeliar" was the name given to the area by the Aboriginal people that lived and hunted in the area.
William F. Buckley Jr. bibliography | This William F. Buckley Jr. bibliography contains a list of works by William F. Buckley Jr.
response.summary_2: William Buckley—the man Strandloper is loosely based on—was given up for dead after escaping transportation and subsequently lived in an Aboriginal community for many years, as corroborated by the William Buckley (convict) entry.
